experienced stigma:
"My insurance provider just denied my request to adjust my medication regimen, claiming it's 'not medically necessary'. But I know it is. The doc even agreed but the nurse practitioner says they don't have 'authorization' to make the change... Like, who even decided that?! 

Does anyone else deal with this kinda nonsense when trying to get adequate treatment? Any advice on what I can do next?

(P.S. if anyone has experience navigating pharmacy denial for insulin prescriptions let me know! That'd be a huge help thanks) 

Tired of getting disbelieved by docs who supposedly are there to HELP"

]
A pharmacist asked me why I don't just "control my blood sugar" without prescribing me any specific advice or solutions.

This pharmacy is now getting one star review on google due to terrible service towards diabetics!

Would like recommendations for diabetes-friendly pharmacies! Who can relate to this and share a better alternative? #DiabetesAwareness #HealthCareInEquality

"I went to get my flu shot today but the pharmacist kept questioning me about taking medication for 'uncontrollable glucose levels'... I'm trying to focus on my physical health but constant comments from healthcare providers and even acquaintances about diabetes management is really demoralizing. #DiabetesStigma"

"My partner has type 2 diabetes and is really struggling. The docs keep being judgy about our weight and saying it's all their fault. I'm just so frustrated and stressed all the time because they don't understand what it's like to live with this constant fear of numbers getting out of control again"

A recent experience made me feel judged by my neighbor: They kept telling me how careless I was to let my kids run around in public when "you have a family member with diabetes". The tone and attitude conveyed was unmistakable – an attempt to make me ashamed of myself.

They failed to recognize that being open about our conditions actually takes immense courage. Not just dealing with personal insecurities, but navigating stigma like ours requires constant strength & determination

What can I say, their disapproval fueled me to talk more openly! My community might see my journey and gain inspiration – which ultimately overcame all stigmas I could be facing today 

Feel empowered to be you because love yourself just the way it is meant!

"My endocrinologist is always trying to increase my insulin dose. She doesn't care that I've been doing extensive research on managing diabetes without medication. The last time I spoke to her, she simply brushed off all of my concerns and told me to focus on avoiding sugar-rich foods... never considering the actual cause of my blood glucose fluctuations."
"Doc didn't believe me that omeprazole was raising my blood sugar. He just shrugged it off like I must be making it up"

My aunt said to my brother that people don't die from diabetes. She said "diabetes isn’t something serious", making a dismissive comment. Her comments really stung. It's like, it may not kill us, but diabetes affects us and it’s an ongoing condition. This sort of behavior makes me sad for those who lack awareness. It was painful, like being slapped by my family members because of something I couldn’t control.

"Today at the hospital, I asked my nurse for some help understanding my medication routine, but she seemed annoyed by all my questions. She said things like 'I've explained this before' and made me feel stupid in front of others waiting in line."

"My friend told me to just 'stop eating sugar'... like that's all I'm doing is indulging in sugary treats. It takes so much work and sacrifice to manage my blood glucose levels, but nobody understands that because they're not living with this invisible burden."
perceived stigma: "Did you know that having diabetes often feels like you're under constant scrutiny from family and friends who don't understand what it means to live with a chronic condition? I've had people imply that my diagnosis is 'all in my head' or that I'm being 'weak' because I need medication. It's time to break free from the stigma surrounding diabetes! Let's create a support network where we can be our authentic selves, share our struggles and triumphs without fear of judgment. Who else has had similar experiences? Share your stories!"

"It's infuriating to see people assume diabetes is just a result of being 'irresponsible'. Not everyone can control their blood sugar, and stigma only adds to the shame and guilt people already feel."

I feel anxious every time I check my A1C levels, wondering if I'll be a slave to insulin and medication like many others. It's not fair that my generation has to live with this constant fear of our health failing us.

"People assume that just because we have diabetes, our baking is now only possible using artificial sweeteners or sugar substitutes. However, many of us prefer traditional ingredients, like refined sugar, which gives it that same taste and feel. Anyone else tired of explaining this to non-diabetic friends?"
"I've been noticing that people always assume I'm eating 'terrible foods' just because I have diabetes. Newsflash: diabetes management is a daily balancing act, not a matter of blame!"

"There's this feeling that everyone in our lives is secretly judging us for taking care of ourselves and making healthy choices with our diabetes. They might be thinking: "They're missing out on all the fun foods, it's just temporary self-deprivation" but the reality is - it's about breaking free from shame, not guilt or shame around food."

"It's like people think I'm reckless or irresponsible because of my Type 1 Diabetes diagnosis. They whisper 'it must have been your lifestyle choices' behind closed doors, not knowing what it means to live with this chronic condition day in and day out. 

They assume they can understand our struggles through one online article or forum post. Meanwhile, we're still navigating these uncertain waters - balancing treatments, emotions, social stigmas, and every possible factor influencing the progression of diabetes."

It's unfair to always have people asking if I've tried a 'specific cure' or telling me it's all about self-control when you live with diabetes.

They seem to forget that managing this condition takes every waking moment, from carefully counting carbs at every meal to navigating emotions like frustration and shame.

But still, some insist we must "just be positive" or have the "right mindset". Newsflash: diabetes isn't just an individual issue; it's about the collective environment, with society imposing limitations and restrictions.

Let's acknowledge that being diagnosed with a lifelong disease requires support rather than judgments!

The perceived stigma surrounding our situation only hinders those seeking real understanding.

- The words that stick with you.
 
We hope for people like [our mods'] approach.

Your responses in these situations would make or break the emotional rollercoaster that people with diabetes live on daily.

I feel anxious every time I think about getting married because I'm a 28-year-old woman diagnosed with Type 2 Diabetes. I've been doing research, but my mom always says that people will view me and my partner as 'that diabetic couple'. They might make comments about us passing on our diabetes to any children we may have. I love kids and the idea of raising a family together, but now I'm hesitant about even having children. Why should I take that risk when others would likely view us as irresponsible?

People seem to have a notion that those with Type 2 Diabetes somehow brought it upon themselves. But research shows there are many factors beyond just diet and exercise at play. It's not just about 'making healthy choices'.
anticipated stigma:
"I've been avoiding sharing my diagnosis with my family because I'm afraid they'll judge me for being diabetic. It's like, they don't understand what it's like to live with a constant check-in on your blood sugar levels all the time. I'm worried that if I open up about this now, they might look at me differently and be 'sorry' or pitying. How do people navigate conversations about diabetes with their family? 

Note: This new post aims to capture anticipated stigma by sharing an experience where someone expects a certain response from others (family members in this case), fearing they will view them with stigma.

"I've been wanting to take control of my diabetes, but I'm nervous about sharing my diagnosis with my new gym instructors. What if they don't understand? What if they make me feel bad about taking time out during a session?"

"I've been wanting to get into rock climbing, but I'm nervous about sharing my Type 1 diabetes with the gym owners in case they think I'm not fit or safe. Has anyone else encountered any issues with telling gyms or fitness studios about their diabetes?"

I've been thinking a lot about when I start telling people that I have diabetes. Right now, everyone is still in shock - including some close friends. The thought of seeing fear, or disgust, or worst case: sympathy all makes me cringe to my stomach. I know they won't get it... at first.

"I've been putting off sharing my Type 1 diabetes diagnosis with my gym instructor because I'm afraid they'll think I'm being lazy or making excuses. Has anyone else struggled with this? How did you bring it up?"

I'm really anxious about getting diabetes because I'm scared of what my friends will think if they see me pricking my finger in public or eating snacks to check my sugar levels. I've always been the one who keeps up with sports and doesn't miss classes, so the thought of needing medication or checking my levels constantly makes me feel like I'll be seen as weak.

"I've been working out at a local gym for months now, but I still haven't told my personal trainer about my t1 diagnosis. We only talk occasionally via messaging app and never in person. They seem like nice people and don't pay much attention to my workouts besides checking my form occasionally. Still feel worried that they'll look at me differently or judge me once they know, even though we mostly chat about routine gym things online."
I've been putting off telling my manager that I have diabetes because I'm worried about how they'll react. Will it make things awkward in the office?
"Working in an office as a freelance writer... still worried that if I 'admit' my type 1 diagnosis they'll fire me."
"I'm dreading the day I'll need to tell my family that I've lost my health insurance... all these fears and anxieties are still too fresh."
internalized stigma: I feel guilty about getting frustrated with my body's slow response to working out. It feels like metformin is hindering my progress, and I worry that it's my own fault for not trying hard enough when I was younger.
"Feeling consumed by self-blame and guilt over a recent blood sugar spike. Like I've undone all my progress and back to square one. Struggling to reconcile with my own mistakes."

I feel so ashamed after being diagnosed as prediabetic despite trying my best to live a healthy lifestyle. It's like I've been living a lie, thinking that all these years of regular exercise and eating "healthy" would protect me from something like this. Now, every bite feels like a risk, and every step on the scale feels like a betrayal. How do others deal with this crushing sense of self-blame?

"I'm ashamed to admit it, but I've been feeling like my prediabetes diagnosis is a personal failing. I exercise regularly, but now I feel guilty after eating sweets because I think it's contributing to my condition. It's like I'm punishing myself for something I can't control."

"I still struggle with depression and self-blame after my diagnosis. I feel like it's my fault that I have diabetes, like if only I had made better choices about food or exercise... Maybe we can relate on being plagued by this sense of regret?"

"I've been beating myself up over my diabetes diagnosis. I keep thinking that if I'd done this or eaten like that, I wouldn't be in this situation. But what's the point? I'll just have to take these pills for the rest of my life, right?"

*The person feels guilty and thinks they could have prevented their diabetes if they had made different choices.

New Post:
"My mum is getting so frustrated with her high blood sugar levels. I don't understand how to help them with a healthy diet. All we ever eat are rice, bread, roti... it's hard for them to switch to complex sugars and avoid carbs."

*The person is confused about diabetes and the role of diet, as well as anxious about being around the diabetes tools that belong to her parents.

New Post:
"I feel so ashamed after giving in to a pastry craving and then checking my blood sugar only to see it was too high. This really isn't what I needed to know, reminding me of how easily one wrong decision can set off my condition... It feels as though there’s a perpetual sense that everything needs be so controlled at every second"

*The person blames themselves for giving in to cravings and getting anxious after seeing their blood sugar was too high, implying an underlying sense of control around their diabetes that can spiral them towards self-blame.

" I feel like it's my fault when I get frustrated and angry when my numbers spike... it takes a toll on me to see the patterns of self-blame. Maybe others out there struggle with it too, maybe we should talk about this shame in quiet times"

"Ever since I received my diabetes diagnosis, I've been carrying around a weight of guilt and self-blame. Why couldn't I prevent this from happening? What if I'd eaten healthier or exercised more often?

Sometimes, even simple things like checking blood sugar levels can trigger those old feelings. Was it all just my own fault?

As someone who's learned to cope with their own body through meditation and acceptance exercises, how do you suggest moving past this pain and internalised stigma? Any experiences of regaining health would mean a great deal."

"I'm still dealing with this internal struggle, I keep telling myself it's my fault, that I'm a burden to others. Like when my loved ones wouldn't come see me, they would say it was my own fault... that guilt lingers inside of me. Trying to convince myself I'm not that kind of person but it's hard."

I'm constantly worrying about going low, like I'm to blame. I measure my blood sugar before every meal, but it's never enough - if it's not perfectly within range, I get anxious and think I've messed up again.

This post reflects the theme of internalized stigma, where the person believes that they are responsible for their condition (being diabetic or prediabetic) and is wracked with guilt and anxiety about controlling their blood sugar levels.

I've always been self-conscious about eating 'incorrectly' even when I know my HbA1c levels show I'm doing alright - why am I beating myself up over this?!

The second example post is not just worried about getting sick, it's more concerned about its diet being poor due to its health issue which was confirmed by psychiatrist also due Arfid, feeling hopeless because their life is changing all because of what other's say or advise
intersectional stigma: "The media portrayal of a fit individual with type 1 diabetes can sometimes overlook intersectional stigmas surrounding health. Let's be real, when it comes to mental illness and racial background. Why are life-style changes for managing blood sugar consistently belittled on these forums? #stigmadead"

"I've seen countless posts saying that pills are the answer, but the real issue is often neglected - our lifestyles. Why do we always focus on medications rather than teaching people how to control their type 2 diabetes through sustainable lifestyle changes? I recently spoke to a colleague who was struggling with their weight and managing his diabetes. The lack of resources for intersectional approaches like healthy eating habits, regular physical activity, and mental well-being strategies for minorities hit me hard.

We should promote more equitable and culturally-sensitive support rather than relying solely on prescription pills that address the symptoms rather than causes."

"I'm fed up with the one-size-fits-all approach on this forum. It's all about pills for a lifestyle disease, but what about real lifestyle changes? Have you ever noticed that thin guys aren't typically type 2 diabetics - it's usually those who are overweight. I've been able to control my own type 2 by making genuine lifestyle adjustments, from 300 down to a still-challenging 160-ish and counting. Can we cut through the misconceptions and industry-funded fakes? 

Types of stigma observed in this example:

- Intersectional stigma

"I'm tired of people thinking that I can control my type 2 diabetes by just popping pills. It's not about that - it's about lifestyle changes, which is stigmatized enough without throwing in societal biases against weight or race. "

"Every piece of advice I read on this forum is just another way to dictate my lifestyle without giving a chance for me to improve through healthy living. Why do thin guys always get blamed for their type 2 diabetes when they've actually got nothing to do with their condition?"

* They said if you are too tall it's an indicator
* Then it comes out its due to being too skinny or eating something else in terms of nutrition

People assume I have schizophrenia because they stereotype individuals with darker skin, but little do they know, I've been managing my mental health through consistent exercise. It's time to break down stigmas around multiple conditions and stop linking one illness to another unjustly.

Why does every health expert still insist on medications rather than encouraging lifestyle changes? 

Have you noticed that people with multiple sclerosis or mental health conditions like schizophrenia are often stereotyped as unhealthy? Meanwhile, someone like a thin CEO who develops type 2 diabetes gets no flak. It's like being labeled a lazy doctor when the truth is different.

Another day, another judgment on my weight. Like I'm defined by my obesity instead of being living with type 2 diabetes. My family and friends make snide comments about what they 'knew' about my diagnosis. Their tone says it all - it's always a question of lifestyle for them. Newsflash: genetics matter too. They should be focused on offering support, not passing the weight stigma. Being thin is not an indication that you don't have diabetes #stigma awareness

What's alarming is that diabetes stigma often intersects with other stigmas. I know someone who has type 2, and the judgment comes from people who assume only obese individuals have this condition. But lifestyle changes can help control it – my uncle, a thin man, brought his levels down to almost normal. Wonder why some folks resist advice on life-style change for a lifestyle disease?

Their diagnosis of schizophrenia often intersects with stigma surrounding mental health issues in some cultures, as it may be viewed as a "western disease".

They have multiple sclerosis, but societal pressure about body shape adds an additional burden, showing how intersecting factors like stigma can affect patient lives.
